data.title: General Dynamics outlines $54.3B–$54.8B revenue target for 2026 while advancing record backlog
data.description:
Earnings Call Insights: General Dynamics Corporation (GD) Q4 2025

Management View

  • Chairperson & CEO Phebe Novakovic highlighted fourth quarter earnings of $4.17 per diluted share on revenue of $14.379 billion, operating earnings of $1.452 billion, and net earnings of $1.143 billion. Novakovic stated that “revenue is up 7.8% and operating earnings are up 2%” year-over-year, emphasizing the strong sequential gains across all financial measures and noting, “the sequential comparisons are quite attractive.” She also underscored that “both revenue and operating earnings were up for each of the segments, led by Marine Systems and Aerospace.”
  • Novakovic described Aerospace as having “continuing strong demand for Gulfstream aircraft, overall strength in Gulfstream service business and continued growth and performance improvement at Jet Aviation.” She reported the delivery of 158 new aircraft, with the G700 and G800 models driving increased demand. On the defense side, Novakovic cited “robust order intake” in Combat Systems, particularly in munitions and European Land Systems, and described Marine Systems as experiencing “exceptional revenue growth,” with a focus on submarine programs and Electric Boat.
  • President Danny Deep explained the $104 million decrease in Aerospace operating earnings was mainly due to three fewer G600 deliveries, lower favorable settlements, higher overhead, and the imposition of tariffs. However, Deep noted, “earnings for the G800 more than replaced the G650 earnings … The G700 also experienced higher earnings despite 2 fewer deliveries.”
  • Deep provided details on key Combat Systems awards, including “over $4 billion for our EAGLE tactical vehicles” in Germany, $600 million in Norway and the UK for bridges, and $640 million in Canada for light armored and logistics vehicles.
  • Senior VP & CFO Kimberly Kuryea said, “Our order activity and backlog continued to be a strong story and a highlight for us in 2025,” reporting a company-wide book-to-bill ratio of 1.5:1 for the year and a record total backlog of $118 billion, a 30% increase over last year. Kuryea also noted, “free cash flow for the year was just shy of $4 billion for a cash conversion rate of 94%.”

Outlook

  • Novakovic projected 2026 Aerospace revenue of about $13.6 billion, operating margin around 14%, and Gulfstream deliveries of 160. For Combat Systems, expected revenue is between $9.6 billion and $9.7 billion with an operating margin of 14.1%. Marine Group revenue is anticipated in the $17.3 billion to $17.7 billion range, with a 30 basis point operating margin improvement. Technologies revenue is expected to reach up to $13.8 billion, with a slight decrease in operating margin.
  • Company-wide, 2026 revenue is forecasted in the range of $54.3 billion to $54.8 billion, with anticipated operating margins of 10.4% and operating earnings around $5.7 billion. EPS guidance is set between $16.10 and $16.20. Novakovic stated, “we feel very good about our business and the prospects for the year.”

Financial Results

  • Fourth quarter revenue was $14.379 billion, with operating earnings of $1.452 billion and net earnings of $1.143 billion. Aerospace segment revenue was $3.788 billion with earnings of $481 million. Combat Systems revenue reached $2.5 billion, up 5.8% year-over-year, with earnings of $381 million. Marine Systems reported revenue of $4.8 billion and operating earnings of $345 million, representing a 21.7% and 72.5% increase, respectively, over the year-ago quarter. Technologies revenue stood at $3.24 billion with operating earnings of $290 million.
  • Year-end backlog hit $118 billion, while company operating cash flow for 2025 totaled $5.1 billion. Capital expenditures were $1.2 billion for the year. Free cash flow for 2025 was “just shy of $4 billion.”

Q&A

  • Seth Seifman, JPMorgan: Asked about Aerospace margins and supply chain impacts. Danny Deep responded that margins are expected to improve through “improved pricing, improved efficiency, some lower overheads and some lower research and development costs.” He noted headwinds from tariffs and supply chain cost increases.
  • Douglas Harned, Bernstein: Inquired about Marine throughput and supply chain constraints. Novakovic explained, “The supply chain remains the gating item … but once they do, that will improve … our productivity and throughput.”
  • Gautam Khanna, TD Cowen: Sought details on tariff impacts at Gulfstream. Deep said, “The impact of tariffs in 2025 was $41 million … tariffs are contemplated in our 2026 margins.”
  • Scott Deuschle, Deutsche Bank: Questioned free cash flow conversion and Navy support. Kuryea said, “strong operating performance out of the business units … are increasing CapEx to a significant extent, but that's factored in.” Novakovic clarified, “Not at this point” regarding Navy working capital support.
  • Sheila Kahyaoglu, Jefferies: Asked about Gulfstream order drivers and Combat Systems capacity. Novakovic cited new product introductions and strong economies. She also stated, “we have capacity and have executed that capacity up in Northeast Pennsylvania at 36 rounds a month … with the capacity of 50,000 rounds a month.”

Sentiment Analysis

  • Analysts were broadly positive, focusing on margin expansion, supply chain recovery, and strong order momentum. Multiple analysts probed for details around tariff impacts, throughput improvements, and capital allocation, signaling continued optimism and pressing for confirmation of sustained growth.
  • Management maintained a confident tone, frequently referencing solid operational execution and robust demand. Novakovic repeatedly highlighted, “we feel very good about our business and the prospects for the year.” During the Q&A, management responses remained measured and detailed, with occasional caution expressed around supply chain and tariff headwinds. Compared to the previous quarter, management’s confidence appeared consistent, with a slight increase in emphasis on operational execution and capital investment.

Quarter-over-Quarter Comparison

  • Guidance increased from a previous EPS forecast of $15.30–$15.35 to $16.10–$16.20 for 2026, with revenue outlook rising from $52 billion to the $54.3–$54.8 billion range. Management continued to emphasize operational performance and productivity improvements, with a greater focus on investments in Marine Systems and Combat Systems.
  • Analysts’ questions shifted from concerns about government shutdown impacts and supply chain stabilization in Q3 to detailed inquiries on the durability of margin expansion, capital expenditures, and order conversion in Q4.
  • Management tone remained confident, but with more explicit discussion of tariffs, supply chain bottlenecks, and investment priorities.

Risks and Concerns

  • Management identified supply chain constraints as the primary risk to productivity and throughput, particularly in Marine Systems and Aerospace.
  • Tariff impacts on Aerospace were cited as a headwind, with Deep noting higher expected tariffs in 2026.
  • Analysts expressed interest in the sustainability of order momentum, capacity expansion, and the timing of new contracts, especially in the defense segments.
  • Novakovic stated, “The supply chain remains the gating item,” while Deep highlighted the need for continued investments and efficiency gains.

Final Takeaway

General Dynamics concluded the year with strong revenue and earnings growth, record backlog, and robust order activity across all major segments. Management projects continued growth for 2026, led by increased revenue targets and EPS guidance, supported by strategic investments in production capacity and operational improvements, while remaining attentive to risks from supply chain and tariffs. The company maintains a focus on execution and investment to sustain momentum into the coming year.

Read the full Earnings Call Transcript

data.title: TrumpRx expected to launch on Friday: report
data.description:
The White House is expected to launch TrumpRx, its newly announced direct-to-consumer platform for pharmaceutical products, on Friday, backed by the recent pricing deals the Trump administration signed with leading drugmakers, according to MS Now.

Citing two White House officials, the media outlet reported that the Trump administration is ready to launch the site following a demonstration at South Court Auditorium of the Eisenhower Executive Office Building.

After reaching a drug pricing deal with Pfizer (PFE) in September, the White House unveiled TrumpRx to offer brand-name prescription medicines at sharply discounted prices for cash-paying customers.

As part of the deal, the New York-based pharma giant agreed to offer discounts ranging from 50% to 85% for some of its blockbuster medicines, including its arthritis drug Xeljanz, in exchange for a three-year tariff reprieve and additional investments in the U.S.

Several other pharma majors, including Eli Lilly (LLY), Novo Nordisk (NVO), AstraZeneca (AZN), EMD Serono of Germany's Merck KGaA (MKGAF), and AbbVie (ABBV), also agreed to offer some of their products on TrumpRx following similar drug pricing deals with the administration.

data.title: WesBanco signals mid-single-digit loan growth for 2026 while expanding health care and southeast LPO strategy
data.description:
Earnings Call Insights: WesBanco, Inc. (WSBC) Q4 2025

Management View

  • CEO Jeffrey Jackson highlighted "successful execution on our growth-oriented business model, while maintaining strong credit quality measures" and reported "full year pretax provision earnings growth of 105% year-over-year and full year earnings per share of 45% to $3.40 when excluding merger-related charges." He emphasized that loan growth was "fully funded by deposit growth, both year-over-year and quarter-over-quarter, helping to drive our fourth quarter net interest margin to $3.61." Jackson also announced the "successful acquisition and integration of Premier Financial, transforming WesBanco into a $28 billion asset regional financial services partner," noting the company now ranks among the top 50 publicly traded U.S. financial institutions based on assets. He pointed to expansion into new markets, including Northern Virginia and Knoxville, a new health care vertical, and the upcoming opening of a Chattanooga financial center, the company's first in Tennessee.
  • Jackson stated, "Our dedicated teams, supported by continued strong customer satisfaction drove deposit growth that fully funded loan growth both year-over-year and quarter-over-quarter."
  • He highlighted record treasury management revenue of $6 million and a record $10.4 billion in wealth management assets under management.
  • CFO Daniel Weiss reported, "For the fourth quarter, we reported GAAP net income available to common shareholders of $78 million or $0.81 per share. And when excluding restructuring and merger-related expenses, fourth quarter net income was $81 million or $0.84 per share." Weiss noted "strong year-over-year pretax pre-provision core earnings growth of 90%" and that "deposits increased 53% year-over-year to $21.7 billion due to acquired PFC deposits of $6.9 billion and organic growth of $662 million which fully funded our loan growth."

Outlook

  • Weiss projected "CRE payoffs to remain elevated during 2026 and currently estimate them to be between $600 million and $800 million for the year, but weighted more towards the first half."
  • He expects the "first quarter net interest margin to be roughly consistent with our fourth quarter margin of 3.61% and then increase 3 to 5 basis points in the second quarter and then modestly grow into the high 3.60% range in the back half of the year."
  • The company anticipates "mid-single-digit year-over-year loan growth during 2026, given the current loan pipeline and the strength of our markets."
  • Branch optimization will yield "the anticipated annual savings of approximately $6 million [which] will begin to be realized midway through the first quarter of 2026."

Financial Results

  • Jackson reported "net income, excluding merger and restructuring expenses available to common shareholders of $81 million and diluted earnings per share of $0.84, which increased 18% year-over-year."
  • Fourth quarter return on tangible common equity was 16%, with nonperforming assets to total assets at 0.33%. CET1 ratio was 10.3%.
  • Total portfolio loans increased 52% year-over-year, to $19.2 billion, including $5.9 billion from the Premier acquisition and organic growth of more than $650 million.
  • Efficiency ratio for the quarter was 52%, and noninterest income for the quarter was $43.3 million.
  • Noninterest expense, excluding restructuring and merger costs, was $144.4 million, up 44% year-over-year due to Premier's expense base and higher FDIC insurance.

Q&A

  • Daniel Tamayo, Raymond James: Asked about the pace of CRE payoffs and loan growth. Jackson responded, "We do believe, just based on our current forecast and talking to customers that it should slow down, especially compared to fourth quarter...loan growth should be in the mid single digits."
  • Tamayo followed up on the health care vertical. Jackson stated, "The team, I believe, did around $500 million in new loans. We had a tremendous amount of deposits and fees...that will be one of the main growth engines of us for this year."
  • Russell Gunther, Stephens: Asked about expense guidance and branch closures. Jackson replied, "We always evaluate the branch network...it is not in any of these numbers, just to be clear. But yes, I think it's safe to say we will definitely evaluate it, and that would be potential addition to reduce our expenses at some point in time this year."
  • Gunther also asked about net interest margin guidance. Weiss explained, "We really kind of effectively pulled forward...the next 3 to 5 basis points that would have otherwise been projected for the first quarter into the fourth quarter, and that's how we get kind of a flatter margin just on a linked quarter basis when we look towards the first quarter."
  • Manuel Navas, Piper Sandler: Asked about loan yield dynamics. Weiss replied, "The weighted average yield on new loans originated in the fourth quarter was right around 6.15%."
  • Catherine Mealor, KBW: Asked about profitability targets. Weiss said, "At a high level...that ROA being right around that [ 130% ] mark, average tangible common equity somewhere in the high teens."

Sentiment Analysis

  • Analysts posed detailed, forward-looking questions on CRE payoffs, margin, expense management, and strategic expansion, with a neutral to slightly positive tone, reflected in the pursuit of granular data for 2026.
  • Management maintained a confident tone, using phrases such as "we feel like," "we believe," and "we feel pretty comfortable," but also acknowledged uncertainties, particularly around the pace of CRE payoffs and loan competition.
  • Compared to the previous quarter, analysts' tone shifted from cautious optimism to a focus on execution risks and the sustainability of growth, while management's tone remained confident and focused on operational discipline.

Quarter-over-Quarter Comparison

  • Guidance language shifted from providing only a near-term fourth quarter view in Q3 to explicit 2026 projections in Q4, including a quantified outlook for CRE payoffs and loan growth.
  • Strategic focus in Q4 highlighted the integration of Premier Financial, expansion into new markets, and the scaling of the health care vertical and LPO strategy, whereas the prior quarter centered more on integration and cost synergies.
  • Analysts in Q4 concentrated on sustainability of loan growth, NIM trajectory, and profitability targets, compared to Q3's questions on integration execution and deposit competition.
  • Management's confidence remained consistent, emphasizing successful execution and robust pipelines, while analysts increased scrutiny on execution risks and the durability of growth drivers.

Risks and Concerns

  • Management cited elevated CRE payoffs as a headwind, with estimates of $600 million to $800 million in 2026, mainly in the first half of the year, and noted its impact on loan growth.
  • The company flagged ongoing pressure from noninterest expense increases due to the Premier acquisition and higher FDIC insurance expense.
  • Provision for credit losses is expected to depend on macroeconomic factors, charge-offs, and loan growth, introducing some uncertainty.
  • Analysts queried about deposit costs, the impact of branch closures, and the sustainability of margin and loan growth.

Final Takeaway

WesBanco closed 2025 with strong organic and acquired growth, disciplined expense management, and robust profitability. The company projects mid-single-digit loan growth for 2026, supported by a strong pipeline, health care vertical expansion, and ongoing southeast market penetration through LPOs and new branches. Management remains focused on operational efficiency, margin improvement, and expense discipline, even as elevated CRE payoffs are anticipated to persist into the first half of the year, and integration of Premier Financial continues to reshape the company's scale and reach.

Read the full Earnings Call Transcript

data.title: Elevance Health targets at least $25.50 adjusted EPS for 2026 as company signals margin recovery and portfolio repositioning
data.description:
Earnings Call Insights: Elevance Health (ELV) Q4 2025

Management View

  • CEO Gail Boudreaux emphasized that "2026 is a year of execution and repositioning," with guidance reflecting "prudent, achievable assumptions, grounded in pricing discipline, operational rigor and targeted investments." She announced 2026 adjusted diluted earnings per share guidance of at least $25.50, noting the 2025 results included approximately $3.75 per share of favorable nonrecurring items.
  • Boudreaux outlined strategic portfolio moves in Medicaid, citing ongoing rate lags versus acuity and utilization, with active engagement on "rate actions and program design changes that support the long-term sustainability of the Medicaid program." She described 2026 as a trough year for Medicaid, expecting an operating margin of approximately negative 1.75%.
  • For Medicare Advantage, Boudreaux reported the company expects membership to decline in the high teens percentage range in 2026 due to "deliberate portfolio actions," while emphasizing the expectation of meaningful margin improvement.
  • The Commercial business was described as maintaining "healthy momentum, particularly in national accounts," with disciplined pricing and a focus on sustainable margins. Boudreaux highlighted the integrated approach, stating, "our integrated approach continues to resonate in the market, and we are pleased that 40 employers over the past 5 years have selected our Anthem-affiliated plans as their sole carrier."
  • Boudreaux noted, "Carelon is increasingly recognized as a differentiated platform in the market with growing demand for its solutions in managing high-cost, complex areas of healthcare," although near-term growth is moderated by lower health plan membership, particularly in CarelonRx.
  • Boudreaux stated, "Our long-term enterprise margin target is 5% to 6%. For Health Benefits, Carelon and CarelonRx, we are targeting mid-single-digit margins with our Carelon Services target unchanged."
  • CFO Mark Kaye reported, "Elevance Health reported adjusted diluted earnings per share of $3.33 for the fourth quarter and $30.29 for the full year." He also highlighted, "Operating revenue for the quarter totaled $49.3 billion, an increase of 10% from the prior year."
  • Kaye described the full year consolidated benefit expense ratio as 90% and noted, "Cost trend development was consistent with our expectations across major lines of business."

Outlook

  • Boudreaux confirmed, "we are establishing 2026 adjusted diluted earnings per share guidance of at least $25.50."
  • Kaye stated, "We anticipate operating revenue to decline in the low single-digit percent range in 2026, driven by a low double-digit percentage decline in risk-based membership, partly offset by higher premium yields and growth in Carelon."
  • The consolidated medical loss ratio is expected to be 90.2%, plus or minus 50 basis points for 2026. Adjusted operating expense ratio is expected at 10.6%, plus or minus 50 basis points.
  • Kaye reaffirmed the long-term expectation "of at least 12% adjusted earnings per share growth annually on average over time," with a return to this level in 2027 off the 2026 earnings baseline.

Financial Results

  • Adjusted diluted EPS for Q4 was $3.33; full year 2025, $30.29.
  • Operating revenue in Q4 was $49.3 billion, a 10% increase year-over-year.
  • Full year operating cash flow was $4.3 billion; Kaye noted expectations for 2026 operating cash flow to be at least $5.5 billion.
  • The company ended the year with 45.2 million members, down approximately 500,000 year-over-year, primarily from Medicaid declines.
  • Share repurchases totaled $2.6 billion for the year; total capital returned (including dividends) was $4.1 billion.

Q&A

  • Albert Rice, UBS: Asked about cost trend assumptions for 2026. Kaye responded with a breakdown across lines, noting Medicaid cost pressure remains at "roughly twice the historical average" and expects some moderation; Medicare cost trend is higher due to membership mix.
  • Andrew Mok, Barclays: Questioned larger-than-expected 2026 membership declines, especially in Medicare. Felicia Norwood explained, "Our enrollment during AEP and the member composition is really aligned with our focus on margin," and that attrition was "very consistent with our strategy." Kaye added Employer Group risk membership is expected to end 2026 down in the high single-digit percent range, driven by "deliberate pricing decisions."
  • Justin Lake, Wolfe Research: Asked for details on Health Benefits margin drivers. Kaye detailed that Medicaid margins were pressured but slightly better than outlook due to favorable prior period development and retroactive rates, and that commercial and ACA cost trends were "very much in line with what we were expecting."
  • Lance Wilkes, Bernstein: Inquired about Medicaid rate outlook. Norwood said, "We are contemplating a composite rate increase in 2026 in the mid-single-digit percent range, net of certain known risk corridor impacts," but rates will still lag trend.
  • Joshua Raskin, Nephron Research: Sought confirmation of the 12% EPS growth target for 2027. Boudreaux explained confidence is due to "multiple independent levers and disciplined execution across Commercial, Medicare, Carelon and Medicaid."
  • Lisa Gill, JPMorgan: Asked about investment timing. Kaye clarified that a portion of planned 2026 investments was pulled forward to 2025 due to tax favorability.
  • Ann Hynes, Mizuho: Questioned changes to long-term margin targets. Kaye said underlying margin expectations for each line of business have not changed, but segment targets are recalibrated to "better reflect the mix of our business today."
  • Christian Borgmeyer, TD Cowen: Asked about ACA membership. Kaye guided to at least 900,000 members at year-end 2026, with open enrollment membership up approximately 10%.
  • Scott Fidel, Goldman Sachs: Asked about 2026 capital deployment and M&A. Kaye indicated a "more conservative posture" on M&A in the near term, prioritizing integration and opportunistic share repurchases.
  • Kevin Fischbeck, BofA: Questioned the strategic implications of broad-based membership declines. Boudreaux replied, "This is a very disciplined approach in each of the businesses," emphasizing strong positioning for sustainable growth.
  • Erin Wright, Morgan Stanley: Inquired about the MA rate notice and policy implications. Boudreaux said, "the advanced notice is effectively flat... and quite frankly, it just doesn't keep pace with the current medical cost and utilization trends."
  • Benjamin Hendrix, RBC: Asked about risk-based solutions in Carelon. Peter Haytaian described a disciplined approach to risk and highlighted new offerings in SMI, oncology, and CareBridge.
  • David Windley, Jefferies: Sought clarification on Medicaid membership decline. Norwood said the 750,000-member decline is "really same store," not reflecting exits from states.
  • Sarah James, Cantor Fitzgerald: Asked about commercial risk attrition and margin guidance. Kaye reiterated high single-digit percent decline in Employer Group risk membership, with Kendrick highlighting strength in ASO and national account growth.
  • Jason Cassorla, Guggenheim: Asked about Carelon margin impacts. Haytaian noted strong external growth offset by affiliated membership attrition and described services and Rx growth rates.
  • George Hill, Deutsche Bank: Sought clarity on "ending baseline" for 2026. Kaye explained the $25.50 EPS guidance is "anchored in...very prudent, achievable assumptions" and detailed key building blocks.

Sentiment Analysis

  • Analysts' tone was neutral to slightly negative, pressing on membership declines, margin expectations, and the sustainability of guidance. They raised several concerns about cost trends, membership attrition, and policy headwinds but did not express alarm, focusing instead on clarity and confirmation.
  • Management maintained a confident but cautious tone in prepared remarks, emphasizing discipline, prudence, and execution. In Q&A, they were direct, sometimes defensive, as shown by Boudreaux's reiteration of "very disciplined approach" and Kaye's detailed breakdowns. The tone was more cautious than the previous quarter, with increased emphasis on margin recovery and sustainable growth.

Quarter-over-Quarter Comparison

  • 2026 guidance is now explicit, with a minimum adjusted EPS target of $25.50, compared to reaffirmed $30 for 2025 in the previous quarter.
  • Management's tone shifted from practical and preparatory in Q3 to more focused on repositioning and margin recovery in Q4.
  • Analysts' questions in Q4 were more centered on membership declines and the impact of deliberate portfolio actions, compared to Q3's focus on investment spending and Medicaid margin deterioration.
  • Strategic focus has evolved from broad-based foundational improvements to targeted actions on margin stabilization, portfolio adjustment, and explicit capital deployment plans with a conservative M&A stance.
  • Key metrics, such as Medicaid operating margin guidance and consolidated medical loss ratio, are now more specific.

Risks and Concerns

  • Management highlighted lagging Medicaid rates versus acuity and utilization, ongoing eligibility reverifications, and policy-driven membership attrition as key risks.
  • There is concern about Medicare Advantage funding not keeping pace with cost trends, which could pressure benefit stability and affordability for seniors.
  • Membership declines, especially in Medicaid and Medicare, present topline headwinds, though management positions these as deliberate portfolio actions for margin improvement.
  • Analysts repeatedly questioned the sustainability of margins, the impact of cost trends, and the prudence of guidance assumptions.
  • Management outlined mitigation strategies including tighter cost management, provider education, targeted program investments, and ongoing state engagement.

Final Takeaway

Elevance Health enters 2026 with a strategy that prioritizes execution, margin recovery, and disciplined portfolio repositioning amid significant industry headwinds. The company is targeting at least $25.50 adjusted EPS for 2026, managing through anticipated declines in Medicaid and Medicare Advantage membership, and recalibrating margin expectations to reflect current market realities. Management expresses confidence in the durability of its diversified platform and its ability to return to at least 12% adjusted EPS growth in 2027, supported by targeted investments, operational rigor, and ongoing cost management initiatives.

Read the full Earnings Call Transcript

data.title: Fed yet to comply with subpoenas as part of Powell probe - report
data.description:
The Federal Reserve has yet to comply with grand jury subpoenas tied to the criminal investigation of Chair Jerome Powell, CNBC reported, citing a person familiar with the matter.

Federal prosecutors recently launched a criminal probe into Powell over the monetary authority's renovation of its Washington headquarters and whether Powell lied to Congress about the project’s scope during his congressional testimony last June.

That testimony concerned a multi-year project to renovate historic Fed office buildings—a matter Powell characterized as merely a “pretext” for the administration’s broader campaign against central bank independence.

Conducted by the office of Jeanine Pirro, the U.S. Attorney for the District of Columbia, the Powell probe remains ongoing, the person told CNBC. 

There is no clear timeline for when the Fed must produce the records sought by the subpoenas.

The Federal Reserve didn't immediately respond to Seeking Alpha's request for comment.

The news comes just hours before the central bank announces its latest interest-rate decision, which is widely expected to be a pause.

data.title: Financial instability risks are rising – Fmr. Atlanta Fed President Lockhart
data.description:
Dennis Lockhart, former president of the Atlanta Federal Reserve, warned that financial instability risks are rising amid a series of significant market moves that have rattled investors in recent weeks.

The veteran central banker expressed concern that these developments are creating stress for the global financial system.

In an interview with CNBC, Lockhart assessed the current market environment and its implications for monetary policy.

“It suggests that financial stability risks are rising,” he said of the recent volatility.

The dollar (DXY) has been trading at its weakest level since 2022, with the sell-off accelerating this year. Meanwhile, gold prices (XAUUSD:CUR) have been hitting new record highs in what Lockhart described as a relentless march higher, and questions have emerged about long-term bonds.

Despite the mounting concerns, Lockhart said the Federal Reserve will likely take a measured approach to the turbulence.

The Fed “does not step in just with every little market upsetting or turmoil,” he explained, adding that he expects the central bank will “wait and see” as conditions develop.

Lockhart noted that the dollar’s value is primarily the U.S. Treasury’s responsibility, not the Fed’s, though coordination between the two institutions exists when necessary. While he acknowledged that the yen (FXY) situation appears to have settled down somewhat, he added that it is “troubling that the global financial system is having some of the stress.”

data.title: U.S. stocks retreat from highs as traders await Fed decision, big tech earnings amid geopolitical turmoil
data.description:
Wall Street's major averages were higher on Wednesday, with the S&P 500 ((SP500)) touching on the 7,000 level in new intraday highs before paring some gains, as investors awaited the Federal Reserve's decision on interest rates and mega-cap tech company earnings.

This came as U.S. President Donald Trump threatened to attack Iran with a “massive armada” if the country does not agree to a deal over nuclear weapons.

The benchmark S&P 500 (SP500) momentarily touched on the 7,000 mark, adding to current highs, and was last -0.1% in afternoon trade. Both the Nasdaq Composite (COMP:IND) and the Dow (DJI) were flat.

“A massive Armada is heading to Iran… It is, ready, willing, and able to rapidly fulfill its mission, with speed and violence, if necessary… Don’t make that happen again,” President Trump said on Truth Social, earlier on Wednesday, referencing a previous military operation he called “Operation Midnight Hammer.”

Traders are also awaiting the Fed's decision this afternoon on interest rates, which are expected to remain at the current rate of 3.5%-3.75%.

“The big question isn’t what the U.S. Federal Reserve will do with rates—the market has priced the probability of a change (i.e., cut) at a de minimis 3%. Instead, the focus is on how central bankers characterize the economic outlook and policy implications,” said Mohamed El-Erian, chief economic adviser at Allianz.

Gold (XAUUSD:CUR) continued reaching new highs, surpassing $5,300 per ounce on Wednesday, while silver (XAGUSD:CUR) reached the $115 mark.

Meanwhile, the U.S. dollar (DXY) fell to its lowest level in almost four years due to geopolitical risks, intervention in the Japanese yen, and the Fed’s interest rate decision.

In addition, major tech company earnings will arrive postmarket, including Microsoft (MSFT), Meta Platforms (META), and Tesla (TSLA).

Over in the bond market, U.S. Treasury yields were virtually unchanged, with the 10-year Treasury yield (US10Y) 1 basis point higher at 4.26%, while the 2-year yield (US2Y) was flat at 3.59%.

“The bond market, precious metals, and currencies seem on edge…there’s a bit of déjà vu with regards to the interplay of the 10-year Treasury yield (US10Y), the dollar index (DXY), and gold (XAUUSD:CUR),” said Jurrien Timmer, director of global macro at Fidelity.

“Tariffs or not, the world order is changing from a dollar-only standard to multiple spheres of influence, and the markets are taking notice. In fact, one could argue that all commodities are becoming strategic assets in this multipolar world,” he added.

data.title: Greenland Technologies prices $6.1M underwritten public offering
data.description:
• Greenland Technologies (Nasdaq: GTEC) on Wednesday said it has priced an underwritten public offering of 5,083,330 units at $1.20 per unit.
  • Each unit consists of one ordinary share and four-fifths of one warrant, with each whole warrant exercisable for one ordinary share at $1.20 per share. The warrants are immediately exercisable and will expire three years from issuance.
  • Gross proceeds from the offering are expected to be about $6.1 million before underwriting discounts and expenses.
  • The company said it plans to use the net proceeds for working capital and general corporate purposes.
  • The offering is expected to close on or about January 29, subject to customary conditions.
  • GTEC is -14.16% to $1.03.
  • Source: Press Release

data.title: Renasant outlines continued efficiency gains and mid-single-digit loan growth target for 2026 amid merger integration
data.description:
Earnings Call Insights: Renasant Corporation (RNST) Q4 2025

Management View

  • Kevin Chapman, President and CEO, stated that 2025 was a transformative year for Renasant, highlighting "considerable improvement in our profitability and strong balance sheet growth on the heels of the completion of the largest merger in the company's history." Chapman emphasized the company's focus on maintaining and accelerating momentum, noting that "Renasant's core profitability showed significant improvement this year, fueled by the benefits of the merger with The First, along with ongoing efforts to improve efficiency at legacy Renasant." Adjusted earnings per share for the year were $3.06, marking an 11% increase year-over-year, and the adjusted efficiency ratio improved by approximately 900 basis points to 57.46%.

  • James Mabry, Executive VP & CFO, reported that "the company's net income was $78.9 million or $0.83 per diluted share. Adjusted earnings, excluding merger charges, were $86.9 million or $0.91 per diluted share." Mabry also highlighted an adjusted return on average assets of 1.29% for the quarter, up 20 basis points from the third quarter, and an adjusted return on tangible common equity of 16.18%. Loans increased by $21.5 million on a linked-quarter basis. Mabry noted loan sales of $117 million of non-core loans and deposit growth of $48.5 million from the third quarter. "Net interest income increased $3.9 million quarter-over-quarter. Reported net interest margin increased 4 basis points to 3.89%, while adjusted margin was flat at 3.62%." Noninterest expense, excluding merger and conversion expenses, was $160.2 million, a decrease of $6.2 million from the prior quarter.

Outlook

  • Management reiterated a mid-single-digit loan growth target for 2026, with Chapman stating, "We're still targeting for the year mid-single digits. And look, I think '26 can be similar to '25, where there might be some lumpiness in the quarters." Mabry addressed expense expectations, indicating that core noninterest expense is expected to decrease by $2 million to $3 million in Q1, consistent with prior guidance.

  • Chapman discussed the balance between cost savings and ongoing investments in talent, explaining that "we're still going to continue to focus on improving profitability and our expenses at Renasant. We're also going to continue to be very focused in making investments for future growth and future profitability."

  • In terms of capital deployment, Mabry said, "the most attractive one to us would be buybacks. And of course, we had some activity in Q4 and would anticipate that activity continues into '26."

Financial Results

  • Net income for the quarter was $78.9 million and adjusted earnings, excluding merger charges, were $86.9 million. Adjusted pre-provision net revenue stood at $118.3 million. The company recorded a credit loss provision on loans of $10.9 million, with net charge-offs of $9.1 million. The allowance for credit losses as a percentage of total loans declined to 1.54%.

  • Noninterest income was $51.1 million, an increase of $5.1 million compared to the prior quarter, including $2 million from the exit of certain low-income housing tax credit partnerships. Net interest income increased by $3.9 million. Noninterest expense, excluding merger and conversion expenses, decreased to $160.2 million, benefiting from $2.1 million in gains from branch consolidations.

Q&A

  • Michael Rose, Raymond James: Asked about the trajectory of expenses and the impact of merger cost savings. Mabry answered that core expenses are expected to come down by $2 million to $3 million in Q1, and salaries showed the most significant impact.

  • Rose followed up on loan growth outlook. Chapman confirmed the mid-single-digit growth target for 2026 and noted that "there is the opportunity for upside as market disruption occurs."

  • Stephen Scouten, Piper Sandler: Questioned the balance between investing in growth and managing expenses. Chapman explained that the company is focusing on accountability and high performance, making selective investments in both production and back office for scalability.

  • Scouten inquired about capital deployment. Mabry stated that organic growth remains the top priority, but buybacks are the most attractive capital lever in the near term. Chapman added that M&A remains on the table if the right opportunity arises.

  • David Bishop, Hovde Group: Asked about the net interest margin outlook. Mabry indicated that margin is expected to be stable in 2026, with modest growth in net interest income as the balance sheet grows.

  • Jordan Ghent, Stephens: Asked for details on the loan sale and whether more sales are expected. Mabry clarified the sale was of loans secured by cash surrender value of life insurance policies and described them as non-core; no further sales are anticipated.

  • Catherine Mealor, KBW: Inquired about buyback levels and CET1 targets. Mabry stated the company aims to end 2026 with a CET1 ratio roughly unchanged from year-end 2025.

  • Sun Young Lee, TD Cowen: Asked if management is satisfied with current profitability metrics or seeking further improvement. Chapman responded that while goals set 18 months ago have been met, "our goal is to continue to improve from here and chase a moving target with the ultimate goal of being high performing."

Sentiment Analysis

  • Analysts pressed for more details on cost saves, capital deployment, and growth outlook, displaying a slightly positive to neutral tone with a focus on sustainability and upside potential.

  • Management's tone was confident and optimistic during prepared remarks, emphasizing momentum and strategic flexibility. During Q&A, management remained consistent, addressing questions directly and reiterating guidance, with Chapman noting, "our execution is what will make the difference against the peer group."

  • Compared to the previous quarter, analyst tone remained attentive to cost and growth, while management showed increased confidence backed by improved results and integration progress.

Quarter-over-Quarter Comparison

  • The current quarter saw management reiterate its mid-single-digit loan growth target and projected further core expense reductions, consistent with the previous quarter's guidance.

  • Efficiency improvements were realized, with the adjusted efficiency ratio improving to 57.46%, alongside higher adjusted return on tangible equity and ROA.

  • The company completed a significant non-core loan sale, which was not present in the previous quarter.

  • Analyst questions continued to focus on operating leverage, cost controls, and capital allocation, with an increased emphasis on capital deployment options such as buybacks.

  • Management's confidence in achieving and exceeding earlier merger-related performance targets was more pronounced this quarter, while analysts continued to seek clarity on recurring and sustainable improvements.

Risks and Concerns

  • Management acknowledged the potential for elevated loan payoffs as a wildcard impacting net loan growth on a quarterly basis, though the full-year outlook remains intact.

  • Competitive pressures, particularly on the deposit side, were highlighted, with Mabry noting, "it's very competitive on both sides. I would say probably a little more competitive incrementally on the deposit side."

  • No additional non-core loan sales are anticipated, and credit quality remains under close watch, but no significant concerns were flagged.

Final Takeaway

Renasant management highlighted a year of transformation and successful integration, achieving targeted improvements in profitability, efficiency, and growth. The company remains committed to further cost reductions and disciplined investments in talent, aiming for mid-single-digit loan growth and stable margins in 2026. Management maintains that ongoing industry disruption creates opportunities for market share gains, while capital deployment will continue to prioritize organic growth and shareholder returns through buybacks. The leadership team expressed confidence in exceeding past targets and pursuing high-performance benchmarks as the company advances into the new year.

Read the full Earnings Call Transcript

data.title: Provident projects 4–6% loan and deposit growth in 2026 while advancing core system conversion
data.description:
Earnings Call Insights: Provident Financial Services, Inc. (PFS) Q4 2025

Management View

  • President and CEO Anthony Labozzetta highlighted that "The Provident team delivered another strong quarter, driven by record revenues, favorable credit metrics and expanding core profitability." The company reported net earnings of $83 million or $0.64 per share for the quarter, with an annualized return on average assets of 1.34% and an adjusted return on average tangible common equity of 17.6%. Labozzetta emphasized record pre-provision net revenue of $111 million and sustained quarterly growth following the Lakeland transaction.
  • Labozzetta reported that the commercial loan team generated total new loan production of $3.2 billion in 2025, resulting in net commercial loan growth of 5.5% for the year. The loan pipeline at year-end stood at $2.7 billion with a weighted average rate of 6.22%, and originations peaked at over $1 billion in the fourth quarter. Core deposits grew $260 million, or 6.6% annualized, compared to the prior quarter.
  • The CEO noted, “We generated record fee revenue of $28.3 million in the quarter,” with Provident Protection Plus and Beacon Trust both cited as drivers of noninterest income growth. Nonperforming assets improved to 0.32%, and share repurchase authorization was announced for up to 2 million additional shares. Preparations are underway for a core system conversion in fall 2026.
  • CFO Thomas Lyons stated, “We reported net income of $83 million or $0.64 per share for the quarter with a return on average assets of 1.34%... Revenue increased to a record for a third consecutive quarter at $226 million, driven by record net interest income of $197 million and record noninterest income of $28.3 million.” Lyons also projected continued core net interest margin (NIM) expansion of 3 to 5 basis points over the next two quarters, with reported NIM estimated in the 3.4% to 3.5% range for 2026.

Outlook

  • Lyons provided guidance for 2026, stating, “We are expecting loans and deposits to grow in the 4% to 6% range, noninterest income to average $28.5 million per quarter and are targeting a core return on average assets in the 120% to 130% range with a mid-teens return on average tangible common equity.”
  • The company expects quarterly core operating expenses of approximately $118 million to $120 million for 2026, with higher run rates in the second half due to the core system conversion and related nonrecurring charges of approximately $5 million. The effective tax rate is projected at approximately 29% for 2026.

Financial Results

  • Provident reported net earnings of $83 million or $0.64 per share, pre-provision net revenue of $111 million, and total revenue of $226 million for the fourth quarter. Net interest income reached $197 million, and noninterest income totaled $28.3 million.
  • Average earning assets grew by $307 million versus the trailing quarter, while the average yield on assets decreased 10 basis points to 5.66%. The cost of interest-bearing liabilities declined 13 basis points to 2.83%, resulting in a core net interest margin increase to 3.01%.
  • Period-end loans held for investment increased $218 million, and period-end deposits rose $182 million compared to the prior quarter. Asset quality remained strong with net charge-offs of $4.2 million and nonperforming assets at 32 basis points of total assets. The allowance coverage ratio was 95 basis points at year-end.

Q&A

  • Mark Fitzgibbon, Piper Sandler: Asked about the timing of tax credit benefits and capital deployment priorities. Lyons responded, “So that was in the Q4. Those are 2025 tax year benefits... Next year’s purchases, the 2026 year will be realized in 2026 as a reduction.” Labozzetta emphasized organic growth as the primary focus, with dividends and potential M&A as secondary considerations.
  • Timothy Switzer, KBW: Inquired about deposit competition, loan repricing, and CRE market trends. Labozzetta said, “Competition is heightening a little bit, but I see the competition for deposits in our market as being universal... we produced nearly $479 million of commercial deposits this year.” Lyons noted, “The total repricing over the next 4 quarters... is about $5.7 billion,” with the rate pickup adding about 4 basis points to NIM.
  • Feddie Strickland, Hovde Group: Explored loan yield potential, fee trends, and geographic expansion. Lyons indicated stable to slightly improving yields, while Labozzetta described ongoing talent additions and geographic penetration as expected.
  • Stephen Moss, Raymond James: Focused on purchase accounting accretion, back book repricing, and hiring plans. Lyons estimated $60 million in purchase accounting accretion for 2026 and confirmed a hiring focus in middle market, insurance, and treasury management.
  • David Storms, Stonegate: Asked about future deposit cost reductions and tech investments. Lyons outlined further potential for lower deposit costs as rate cuts flow through, and Labozzetta detailed the upcoming core systems conversion as a major tech initiative.

Sentiment Analysis

  • Analysts maintained a neutral to slightly positive tone, congratulating management and focusing on operational execution, competitive pressures, and growth strategies.
  • Management’s tone was confident and forward-looking in both prepared remarks and Q&A. Labozzetta and Lyons repeatedly emphasized sustained organic growth, ongoing investments, and strong credit quality, using statements like, “We are pleased with our organic growth momentum and improved profitability.”
  • Compared to the prior quarter, the tone remained constructive, with management expressing increased confidence in growth initiatives and operational leverage.

Quarter-over-Quarter Comparison

  • Guidance language shifted to explicit projections for 2026, including a 4% to 6% growth target for both loans and deposits, and new details on the system conversion timeline.
  • Strategic focus continues to center on organic growth, expansion in middle market and fee-based businesses, and technology upgrades.
  • Analysts’ questions this quarter were more focused on capital deployment, competitive pressures, and specifics of the upcoming system conversion, compared to last quarter’s emphasis on loan portfolio mix and operational efficiency.
  • Key metrics improved, including record revenue, pre-provision net revenue, and noninterest income, while asset quality remained strong. Management confidence in sustaining performance and executing investment initiatives increased, as evidenced by more detailed forward guidance and system conversion plans.

Risks and Concerns

  • Management cited ongoing competition for deposits as a challenge, noting, “everybody is in a fight for... low-cost money.”
  • The company addressed CRE market health and exposure, emphasizing that New York City rent-stabilized multifamily exposure is less than 1% of total loans and remains performing.
  • Analysts raised concerns about future deposit cost reductions, CRE portfolio acquisitions, and loan repricing risk, with management indicating mitigation through talent investments, product diversification, and disciplined underwriting.

Final Takeaway

Provident Financial Services concluded the quarter with record profitability, robust loan and deposit growth, and steady asset quality. The company is projecting 4% to 6% growth in loans and deposits in 2026, supported by new investments in talent, platforms, and a major system conversion. With continued focus on organic growth, disciplined credit, and operational efficiency, management remains confident in achieving its strategic and financial objectives for the coming year.
